profile.name: Tobi Awolaju
profile.title: Full-Stack Engineer — Onchain Applications & Agent Infrastructure
profile.bio:
"I build infrastructure that survives chain chaos"

What I Bring to a Team:
• Full-Stack Onchain Applications: From React frontends with embedded wallet onboarding to WebSocket game servers syncing 20-player 3D state at 20Hz — I own the full stack.
• EVM Infrastructure: Built a reorg-resistant block indexer that cross-references three Monad nodes in real-time and auto-rolls back state on parent-hash mismatches.
• Agent Systems: Autonomous pipelines that self-evaluate, self-retry, and execute without a human in the loop — from a headless PC-controlling agent to a full relay engine.

projects.title: MoyakiTraceEngine ↗
projects.description: A resilient EVM block indexer built for chains that reorganize. Cross-references three Monad nodes in real-time and automatically rolls back state when parent-hash mismatches are detected.

writeup.goal: Build a resilient EVM indexer that never drops data, even when the blockchain reorganizes.
writeup.hardPart: Public RPCs are unreliable. I built a multi-node ingestion engine that cross-references 3 different Monad nodes, with a custom 'ReorgManager' that detects parent-hash mismatches and automatically rolls back bad state.
writeup.stack: Node.js, Express, Svelte, Firebase RTDB, EVM RPCs.

projects.title: World of Nads ↗
projects.description: 20-player browser battle royale — 3D assets, 20Hz WebSocket server, on-chain prize distribution, and embedded wallet onboarding, all running in the browser with no install. Core mechanics validated on Monad testnet; currently porting the settlement layer to Solana.

writeup.overview: A solo experiment in end-to-end creation: 3D modeling, smart contracts, and real-time multiplayer architecture.
features: Low-friction Web3 onboarding via embedded wallets
writeup.architecture: Godot → WASM + React, Node.js WebSocket server @20Hz, EVM (Monad) smart contracts — Solana port (Anchor) in progress.
writeup.infrastructure: High-performance deployment on Vercel and Render.
writeup.techStack: Godot, TypeScript, Solidity, Rust, Anchor, Solana, Monad, Node.js, PostgreSQL.
writeup.web3: Real-time state sync coupled with Privy for seamless identity and on-chain payouts.
writeup.challenges: Solving the 'impossible' task of keeping 20 players perfectly synced while handling blockchain state.
writeup.outcomes: A live, functional product used by real people, proving that a solo builder can ship a complete Web3 ecosystem.
writeup.future: Expanding the physical laws of the game, native Solana settlement program, and WONs2 decentralized relay network.

projects.title: Noogler - Embodied AI ↗
projects.description: An AI agent that controls a PC through screen capture and a Rust OS input layer — not APIs. It sees the interface, makes decisions, and clicks. Like a human, but headless.

writeup.overview: An obsession with 'Digital Agency': building an agent that can operate software autonomously by observing and interacting.
features: Real-time screen capture and visual reasoning
Gemini 2.0 Live API for high-level decision making
Rust-based local agent for direct OS-level control
writeup.architecture: Node.js Cloud Backend + Gemini 2.0, Svelte UI, and a local Rust agent for automation.
writeup.infrastructure: Local automation layer optimized for Windows environments.
writeup.challenges: Ensuring the agent can handle errors and navigate diverse software interfaces autonomously.
writeup.outcomes: A working prototype that can fix its own errors in IDEs, Excel, and browsers.
writeup.future: Advancing toward a fully autonomous companion that learns from user behavior.

projects.title: HARE — Headless Autonomous Relay Engine ↗
projects.description: Agent infrastructure that removes the human from both loops — execution and quality control. Tasks self-evaluate, self-retry, and emit trusted results with no human in the chain.

writeup.goal: Build the foundation for agents that are autonomous in the way that actually matters — no human reviewing outputs, no human retrying failures, no human deciding if the result was good enough.
writeup.hardPart: Most 'autonomous' agent frameworks are autonomous in name only. They remove the human from execution but quietly keep them in the quality control loop. HARE treats reliability as a first-class architectural constraint. Tasks must self-evaluate against a success condition, self-retry with adjusted context on failure, and emit structured results that downstream systems can trust without human inspection.
writeup.stack: Node.js, TypeScript, LLM APIs, Webhook Infrastructure.

projects.title: DominusQuant: Monad Keeper & Algo-Trading Bot ↗
projects.description: Algo-trading infrastructure for Monad. Parses confirmed on-chain state, gas pricing logs, and behavioral signals in real-time — executing only when the statistical edge is there.

writeup.goal: Automate complex DeFi strategies (like liquidations, statistical arbitrage, and tokenomic rebalancing) on a high-speed chain.
writeup.hardPart: Monad's architecture is designed to prevent traditional mempool sniping and sandwich attacks. To find an edge, I had to build a system that parses confirmed on-chain state, gas pricing logs, and AI-aggregated behavioral signals in real-time to execute trades strictly based on speed and statistical advantage.
writeup.stack: TypeScript, Monad Situation Dashboard APIs, Distributed Node.js Microservices, Firebase.

projects.title: LockedIn
projects.description: A day planner with a natural language query layer. Block your schedule, ask it questions in plain English, and push the whole thing to Google Calendar — no copy-paste, no drift.

writeup.goal: Build a planner that closes the gap between intention and execution — not with motivation features, but with structure. If your day is scheduled with times and durations, the friction to skip it goes up. That's the whole mechanic.
writeup.hardPart: Natural language scheduling is easy to demo and hard to make reliable. The query layer has to understand temporal relationships ("what's after lunch?", "when's my first gap?") without a rigid command syntax, then map those results back to a mutable schedule state. Getting that to sync correctly with Google Calendar — without duplication, without drift — required treating the calendar as a write-only commit target, not a two-way sync partner.
writeup.stack: TypeScript, JavaScript, Google Calendar API, Natural Language Query Layer.

docs.title: Communicating in Functions↗
docs.description: On engineering as communication — and why a function is just an idea precise enough to survive without its author.

docs.title: Success is 90% luck and informed guessing. Success is 100% luck if you're uninformed. ↗
docs.description: A probability framework for luck, consistency, and what to do when life hands you a defective die.
